notes: Eight-factor canonical structure generated for deterministic diagnosis flow.
Controlled vocabulary and synonym dictionary are included for normalization.
Clue strength is heuristic and should be calibrated with evaluation data in later phases.

Tubers may show symptoms ranging from slight vascular discolouration to soft rot of entire tuber. Typicallyinfected tubers have soft rot in the pith or medullary region of the tuber extending into the tuber from the stolon end
Cool wet soil at planting followed by high temperature after emergence, Tubes harvested atsoil temperatures of 20-25 C are susceptible

Small brown to black necrotic lesions on stem

Small spots, dry and papery in texture in lower and older leaves. The spots expand becoming brown to black circular to ovoid lesions often appearing to have concentric rings giving it a target boar like appearance. The tissue around the lesions is often chlorotic. As the disease advances the entire leaves become chlorotic and then nerotic and the leaves dry but do not abscise.
Dark sunken circular to irregular shaped lesions accompanied by raised border of purplish to gun metal colour. The underlying tissue is dry leathery to corky and usually brown.
Alternating wet and dry weather, prolonged period of dew, poor nutrition or other stresses temperature range 24-29 C.

abiotic_context: Alternating wet and dry weather, prolonged period of dew, poor nutrition or other stresses temperature range 24-29 C.

crinkled leaflets with Chlorotic or necrotic margins

evidence: Brown lesions extending from seed tuber, pith dark and cortical decay

Tan brown to black lesions whih are normally circular. Lesions may be superficial cork like or pitted. Pitted lesions are dark bown to almost black. Tissue under lesion straw coloured and somewhat translucent.
Extensive brown to black necrosis
Necrotic lesions without concentric zonation black when wet and brown when dry with or without sparse white sporulation possibly yellow halo
Exterior of infected tubers show irregular small to large slightly depressed areas of brown to purplish skin. The boundary line between diseased and healthy tissue is not clearly defined
Temp, RH
Warty outgrowth or tumourous Galls at the base of the stem. Above ground galls may appear green to brown
Below ground galls appear on stem bases, stolons and yes of tubers. Subterranean galls are white to brown and become black due to decay
Galls on tips
Cool temperatures with an average of 18 C, about 160 days of winters with temperatures < 5 C and annual precipitation of 70 cm favour the disease

evidence: Upper leaves light green Interveinal chlorosis later turns yellow to white, lower leaves not affected, leaves near shoot tip roll upward, in severe cases brown necrotic spots develop along the veins of younger leaves

evidence: Mild to severe rugosity
Round brown necrotic lesions may have concentric rings like that in early blight but the spots are not sunken.

Central tissue appears water soaked or translucent followed by formation of one cavity at the centre of the tuber, cavities are lens or star shaped and angular at the corners, Centre hollow or cracked
